message.content:
Review the RFC document at /Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/pigment/docs/rfc/1-trending-products-widget-migration.md

This is a design RFC for migrating a TrendingProducts widget from a catalog app into the Pigment design system (a React component library at CustomInk). The repo is at /Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/pigment.

Read the full RFC, then read the following files for context:
- /Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/catalog/app/javascript/features/TrendingProducts/TrendingCarousel.jsx
- /Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/pigment/packages/react/src/v0/components/Carousel/Carousel.tsx
- /Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/pigment/packages/react/src/v0/components/ProductRecommendationCard/ProductRecommendationCard.tsx
- /Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/pigment/packages/react/src/components/Button/Button.tsx (to understand v1 patterns)
- /Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/pigment/docs/ai/existing-patterns/components.md

Do a thorough review covering:

1. **Completeness** — Is anything missing that a developer would need to implement this? Any gaps in the contracts?
2. **API design** — Are the prop APIs clean, consistent, and follow Pigment v1 patterns? Anything awkward or inconsistent?
3. **Technical correctness** — Any issues with the hook implementation, GA4 event shapes, carousel approach, AbortController usage?
4. **Architecture** — Does the fast-track / Atoms backfilling approach make sense? Any risks?
5. **Contradictions or inconsistencies** — Does anything contradict itself across sections?
6. **Naming** — Component names, prop names, file paths — anything that should be renamed?

Be direct and specific. Point to exact section/line where issues exist. Separate clear problems from suggestions.

content.text: I'll read all the relevant files to give a thorough review.

content.content:
1→# Component Source Code Patterns 2→ 3→> Reference for implementing new Pigment components. Extracted from migrated components (Button, Link, Typography, ScreenReadersOnly). 4→ 5→--- 6→ 7→## File Structure & Naming 8→ 9→**Component folder structure:** 10→``` 11→packages/react/src/components/ 12→├── ComponentName/ 13→│ ├── ComponentName.tsx # Main component 14→│ ├── ComponentName.module.css # CSS Modules styles 15→│ ├── ComponentName.test.tsx # Unit tests (optional) 16→│ └── index.ts # Barrel exports 17→``` 18→ 19→**Naming conventions:** 20→- PascalCase for files: `Button.tsx`, `Link.tsx` 21→- Suffix `.module.css` for styles 22→- Suffix `.test.tsx` for Jest tests 23→ 24→**Barrel exports (index.ts):** 25→```typescript 26→export { Button, type ButtonProps } from './Button'; 27→``` 28→ 29→**Main package index.ts:** 30→```typescript 31→export { ComponentName } from './components/ComponentName'; 32→export type { ComponentNameProps } from './components/ComponentName'; 33→``` 34→ 35→--- 36→ 37→## React Patterns 38→ 39→### Simple Component Structure (Preferred) 40→ 41→For most components without polymorphism. 42→ 43→> **Note:** The code below is a simplified example illustrating the pattern structure, not a literal template. Adapt naming, props, and logic to your component's needs. 44→ 45→```typescript 46→import React from 'react'; 47→import styles from './Component.module.css'; 48→ 49→interface ComponentProps { 50→ /** Description with @default */ 51→ variant?: 'primary' | 'secondary'; 52→ children?: React.ReactNode; 53→} 54→ 55→/** 56→ * Component description. 57→ */ 58→export const Component = React.forwardRef( 59→ ({ variant = 'primary', children, className, ...restProps }, ref) => { 60→ const allClassNames = [styles.root, styles[variant], className] 61→ .filter(Boolean) 62→ .join(' '); 63→ 64→ return ( 65→
66→ {children} 67→
68→ ); 69→ } 70→); 71→ 72→Component.displayName = 'Component'; 73→``` 74→ 75→### Complex Polymorphic Pattern (When Needed) 76→ 77→Use this pattern **only** when the component needs: 78→- A `component` prop to change the rendered element type 79→- Generic props that vary based on the element type 80→- Properly typed refs for each element type 81→ 82→**When to use:** Button (can be `

1→import React from 'react'; 2→import styles from './Button.module.css'; 3→ 4→// Base Button props interface 5→interface BaseButtonProps { 6→ /** 7→ * The *Pigment* variant of the button to use. 8→ * @default 'primary' 9→ */ 10→ variant?: 'primary' | 'secondary' | 'tertiary' | 'link'; 11→ /** 12→ * The size of the button. 13→ * @default 'medium' 14→ */ 15→ size?: 'small' | 'medium' | 'large'; 16→ /** 17→ * Element placed before the children. 18→ */ 19→ startIcon?: React.ReactNode; 20→ /** 21→ * Element placed after the children. 22→ */ 23→ endIcon?: React.ReactNode; 24→ /** 25→ * If `true`, the button is disabled. 26→ * @default false 27→ */ 28→ disabled?: boolean; 29→ /** 30→ * If `true`, the button will take up the full width of its container. 31→ * @default false 32→ */ 33→ fullWidth?: boolean; 34→ /** 35→ * The component used for the root node. 36→ */ 37→ component?: React.ElementType; 38→ /** 39→ * If provided, the button will render as a link. 40→ */ 41→ href?: string; 42→ /** 43→ * Button content 44→ */ 45→ children?: React.ReactNode; 46→} 47→ 48→// Helper type to extract element type from component prop 49→type ElementFromComponent = T extends keyof JSX.IntrinsicElements 50→ ? JSX.IntrinsicElements[T] extends React.DetailedHTMLProps, infer E> 51→ ? E 52→ : never 53→ : T extends React.ComponentType> 54→ ? T extends React.ForwardRefExoticComponent 55→ ? ComponentProps extends React.RefAttributes 56→ ? RefType 57→ : never 58→ : never 59→ : never; 60→ 61→// Polymorphic component props with proper ref inference 62→export type ButtonProps = BaseButtonProps & 63→ Omit, keyof BaseButtonProps> & { 64→ component?: TComponent; 65→ }; 66→ 67→// Internal component function 68→function ButtonImpl( 69→ { 70→ component, 71→ href, 72→ variant = 'primary', 73→ size = 'medium', 74→ startIcon, 75→ endIcon, 76→ disabled = false, 77→ fullWidth = false, 78→ children, 79→ className, 80→ style, 81→ tabIndex, 82→ type, 83→ ...restProps 84→ }: ButtonProps, 85→ ref: React.ForwardedRef>, 86→) { 87→ // Determine if this should be a link 88→ const isLink = typeof href === 'string'; 89→ const Component = component || (isLink ? 'a' : 'button'); 90→ 91→ // Build CSS class names using CSS modules 92→ const rootClassNames = [styles.button, styles[variant], styles[size], fullWidth && styles.fullWidth, className] 93→ .filter(Boolean) 94→ .join(' '); 95→ 96→ // Props for the component 97→ const componentProps = { 98→ ref, 99→ className: rootClassNames, 100→ style, 101→ disabled: Component === 'button' ? disabled : undefined, 102→ 'aria-disabled': disabled ? true : undefined, 103→ href: isLink ? href : undefined, 104→ tabIndex: disabled ? -1 : tabIndex, 105→ type: Component === 'button' ? type || 'button' : undefined, 106→ ...restProps, 107→ }; 108→ 109→ return React.createElement( 110→ Component, 111→ componentProps, 112→ <> 113→ {!!startIcon && {startIcon}} 114→ {children} 115→ {!!endIcon && {endIcon}} 116→ , 117→ ); 118→} 119→ 120→// Polymorphic component interface with proper ref typing 121→interface ButtonComponent { 122→ // Default button element 123→ (props: ButtonProps & React.RefAttributes): React.ReactElement | null; 124→ 125→ // Explicit overloads for common cases with proper ref types 126→ ( 127→ props: ButtonProps & React.RefAttributes, 128→ ): React.ReactElement | null; 129→ 130→ ( 131→ props: ButtonProps & React.RefAttributes, 132→ ): React.ReactElement | null; 133→ 134→ ( 135→ props: ButtonProps & React.RefAttributes, 136→ ): React.ReactElement | null; 137→ 138→ // Generic case for any other element type 139→ ( 140→ props: ButtonProps & React.RefAttributes>, 141→ ): React.ReactElement | null; 142→ 143→ displayName?: string; 144→} 145→ 146→/** 147→ * Pigment Button component with Figma design system implementation. 148→ * Maintains API compatibility with v0 Button for easy migration. 149→ * 150→ * @example 151→ * ```tsx 152→ * // Default as button 153→ * 154→ * 155→ * // As anchor with proper href typing 156→ * 157→ * 158→ * // As div with proper div props 159→ * 160→ * 161→ * // With refs that have correct types 162→ * const buttonRef = useRef(null) 163→ * const linkRef = useRef(null) 164→ * 165→ * 166→ * ``` 167→ */ 168→export const Button: ButtonComponent = React.forwardRef(ButtonImpl) as ButtonComponent; 169→ 170→Button.displayName = 'Button'; 171→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. content.text: Now let me check the package index to understand the current export pattern, and also look at where widgets would fit in the package structure:

Now I have everything I need for a thorough review. Here is my analysis:

---

## RFC Review: Trending Products Widget Migration

### Summary

The RFC is well-structured and covers the broad strokes correctly. The fast-track / Atoms backfilling approach is sound. However there are a number of concrete problems — some blocking, some advisory — spread across the API contracts, hook implementation, technical assumptions, and structural decisions.

---

## 1. Completeness

**Missing: No Storybook requirement mentioned.**
The CLAUDE.md and `components.md` patterns document make clear that Storybook documentation is a success criterion and expected deliverable for all components. The RFC says nothing about Storybook stories for any of the 7 new artifacts. This is a gap that will cause friction at review time. Each atom going into `Atoms/` needs at minimum a story to be usable by other teams. Whether that story lives in Storybook or is deferred should be an explicit decision, not silence.

**Missing: No test requirement mentioned.**
The `components.md` pattern doc specifies co-located `.test.tsx` files for every component. The RFC makes no mention of tests for any of the atoms, the widget, or the hook. For a library component this is a blocker; it should at least acknowledge the expectation.

**Missing: No `.module.css` files mentioned anywhere.**
Every v1 component in `packages/react/src/components/` has a `.module.css` file. The RFC folder structure (`Section 1`) lists only `.tsx`, `.ts`, and `index.ts` files under `Atoms/`. There is no mention of CSS modules or design tokens usage. This matters especially for the `Carousel`, `FavoriteButton`, `Tooltip`, and `Popover` which have non-trivial visual styling. If the plan is inline styles or emotion/MUI `styled`, that contradicts v1 patterns. If it is CSS modules, it needs to be in the folder structure.

**Missing: No `forwardRef` requirement mentioned.**
`components.md` states: "All components MUST use `React.forwardRef`. No exceptions." None of the prop type contracts in sections 4.2–4.6 address this. A developer reading this RFC would not know they need `forwardRef` on every atom.

**Missing: Export path not defined.**
The RFC says the catalog will import `{ TrendingProductsWidget, FavoriteButton } from '@customink/pigment-react'` (Section "Assembled widget", usage example line 2). But the widget lives at `packages/react/src/widgets/...`, not `packages/react/src/components/...`. The `src/index.ts` currently only exports from `src/components/`. There is no statement about how these widgets get added to the public package index, or whether they get a separate entrypoint (e.g. `@customink/pigment-react/widgets`). This is a real implementation gap — developers can't consume the widget without this being resolved.

**Missing: Dependency declarations.**
The RFC proposes adding `@floating-ui/react` as a new dependency for Tooltip and Popover. No mention of adding it to `packages/react/package.json`. The v0 `Carousel` already depends on `keen-slider` and `@react-hook/resize-observer` — the RFC should explicitly confirm those are already in the dep tree and available to v1 code, or call out that they need adding.

**Missing: `imageAlt` prop.**
The v0 `ProductRecommendationCard` accepts `imgAlt`. The catalog's `RelatedProductCard` uses `name` as `alt`. The RFC's `RecommendationsProductCardProps` (Section 4.3) has `imageSrc` and `imageSrcSet` but no `imageAlt`. Accessibility requires a meaningful `alt`. Using `name` as a default is fine but needs to be stated. Without it a developer will either omit `alt` (accessibility failure) or add a prop that wasn't specced.

**Missing: `loop` behavior clarification for Carousel.**
The catalog `TrendingCarousel.jsx` hardcodes `loop: true` in the KeenSlider config (line 157). The RFC `CarouselProps` exposes `loop?: boolean` with no default stated. The widget table says "Arrows shown only on desktop when `slidesPerView !== 'auto'`" but does not say whether the widget passes `loop` to the carousel or leaves it at the prop default. This will silently differ from the catalog behavior if the default is `false`.

---

## 2. API Design

**`Carousel.onPreviousClick` / `onNextClick` are notification-only, but there is no way to control the active slide.**
The prop contract (Section 4.2) exposes `onPreviousClick` and `onNextClick` as callbacks. These mirror the v0 `Carousel`. But the v0 `Carousel` calls these *after* the slide change — they are notifications, not interceptors. The RFC does not clarify whether these are pre- or post-navigation. The catalog's `TrendingCarousel.jsx` (lines 208–221) calls `instanceRef.current.prev()` directly and does not use these callbacks at all. If the v1 Carousel is truly meant to be a drop-in for the trending widget, the widget itself must call into the carousel instance. The current prop contract does not expose a `ref` to the underlying keen-slider instance, which means the widget cannot imperatively control the carousel unless the carousel internally wires `onPreviousClick` to `prev()`. This should be stated explicitly.

**`Carousel.arrows?: boolean` default not stated.**
The v0 `Carousel` defaults `arrows` to `true`. The v1 contract leaves the default undefined. The widget logic says "Arrows shown only on desktop when `slidesPerView !== 'auto'`" — meaning the widget conditionally passes `arrows`. But if `arrows` defaults to `true`, then omitting it renders arrows unconditionally. This needs a default.

**`Carousel.spacing` naming inconsistency.**
The v0 `Carousel` calls this prop `slidesSpacing`. The RFC renames it to `spacing` (Section 4.2). That is a fine improvement, but the usage example in the widget table uses `spacing={16}` while the prop type definition also says `spacing`. This is internally consistent — just noting the break from v0 for migration awareness.

**`RecommendationsProductCard` types use `interface` keyword indirectly via naming but the RFC does not specify.**
`components.md` states: "Use `type` declarations. Avoid `interface` declarations." The RFC uses `type` for all its contracts. That is correct.

**`RecommendationsProductCard.onClick` type is `React.MouseEventHandler` but the card contains a link.**
The card's root element wraps content in an `<a href={linkHref}>`. If `onClick` fires on the outer card, the link will also fire its default navigation. The catalog's `RelatedProductCard` (line 53) puts `onClick` on the `<Card>` wrapper while the `<a href={linkHref}>` is a child. This means a card click fires both the `onClick` handler and triggers link navigation. The RFC does not address this interaction. The usage example (lines 170–172) calls `() => trackClick(product)` inside `onClick`, which is fine for analytics, but the implementer needs to know not to `e.preventDefault()` if link navigation should proceed. This interaction should be documented in the contract.

**`FavoriteButton.onToggle` signature is unnecessarily broad.**
`onToggle: (event: React.MouseEvent | React.KeyboardEvent) => void` — the keyboard case for a `<button>` element is normally handled natively (spacebar/enter fire the `click` event). Using `React.KeyboardEvent` here implies the component manually handles `keydown`/`keyup`. If it does, that should be stated. If it does not, the union type is misleading. The catalog's `FavoriteButton` only uses `onClick`. Consider `onClick?: React.MouseEventHandler` unless keyboard events are explicitly handled separately.

**`Tooltip` is missing `className` and `style` props.**
Every other atom in the RFC includes the escape-hatch `className?: string` and `style?: React.CSSProperties`. Section 4.5's `TooltipProps` omits both. This is inconsistent with the stated Pigment pattern.

**`TrendingProductsWidget` has both `renderFavoriteButton` and `renderCardContent`, plus `children`.**
Three different extension points on one component is a lot. `children` is used in the usage example for a "View all" link that appears outside the carousel (lines 406–407 in the catalog usage example). But `renderCardContent` appears to render inside each card (mirroring `children` on `RecommendationsProductCard`). The RFC does not explain where `children` on the widget renders. Is it below the carousel? Above the title? This needs a layout diagram or explicit description. Without it, different developers will assume different things.

**`ga4ListName` default is stated as "widget title" but the widget title is optional.**
The `TrendingProductsWidgetProps` says `title?: string` — optional. Then `ga4ListName` default is described as "widget title." If `title` is undefined and `ga4ListName` is also undefined, what value ends up in the GA4 event? This is a silent analytics data quality issue.

---

## 3. Technical Correctness

**`useTrendingProducts` — `enabled` default comment is wrong.**
Section "useTrendingProducts hook", the JSDoc comment says `@default true` for `enabled`. But the comment in the code reads `/** Skip fetching. @default true */`. "Skip fetching" describes the semantics of `enabled = false`, not `enabled = true`. The comment directly contradicts the default. The comment should read: "Whether to fetch. @default true" or similar.

**`useTrendingProducts` — `fetcher` in the `useEffect` dependency array will cause infinite re-fetches.**
The hook's `useEffect` dependency array is `[fetcher, categoryId, enabled]` (line at the bottom of the hook body). If the consuming code passes an inline arrow function as `fetcher`:
```tsx
useTrendingProducts({
  fetcher: (categoryId) => fetchTrendingByCategory(categoryId),
  categoryId: props.categoryId,
})
```
...that arrow function is recreated every render. The effect will fire on every render, making the cache pointless and potentially hammering the API. The "Usage in catalog" example at the bottom of the hook section does exactly this. The RFC needs to either (a) require that consumers memoize `fetcher` with `useCallback`, (b) use a stable ref pattern internally, or (c) acknowledge this and recommend that catalog wraps the fetcher with `useCallback`. This is a correctness bug in the RFC's own example.

**`useTrendingProducts` — `isLoading` is not initialized to `true` when `enabled = true`.**
When `enabled` is true and there's no cache, the first render returns `{ products: [], isLoading: false, isError: false }` before the effect fires. The effect then sets `isLoading = true`. This means on mount there is one render with `isLoading: false` and an empty product list — which would cause the widget to return `null` (since `products.length === 0`) before the skeleton appears. The RFC's own widget description says "Loading state renders 4-card shimmer skeleton" — but the hook would not report `isLoading: true` on that first render. This should initialize `isLoading` to `true` when `enabled` is `true` and there's no cache hit to check at init time.

**`useTrendingProducts` — cache across unmount/remount.**
The cache is a `useRef`, which means it is per-component-instance. If the `TrendingCarousel` unmounts and remounts (e.g. during navigation), the cache is gone and the fetch fires again. This is probably acceptable behavior for now, but it should be explicitly noted since it differs from a module-level cache. If multiple widget instances on the same page share the same `categoryId`, each will fetch independently. The RFC does not mention this.

**Carousel — `ResizeObserver` dependency not addressed.**
The catalog's `TrendingCarousel.jsx` uses `@react-hook/resize-observer` (line 10) and the v0 `Carousel` also uses it (line 4 of `Carousel.tsx`). The RFC does not mention this dependency at all for the v1 Carousel. Skipping `ResizeObserver` in v1 will cause the carousel to not update when its container is resized (e.g. panel collapse, responsive layout shift). This was specifically called out in the v0 Carousel comment at lines 86–93.

**Carousel — `'auto'` slide width mode is not implemented in v0 and the RFC does not explain the keen-slider API for it.**
The v0 `Carousel` uses `perView: slidesPerView` which does not support `'auto'`. The catalog `TrendingCarousel.jsx` bypasses Pigment's v0 Carousel entirely and uses KeenSlider directly with `slidesPerView: 'auto'` (line 163). The RFC's v1 `CarouselProps` accepts `slidesPerView?: number | 'auto'` but does not explain how `'auto'` maps to keen-slider's API. In keen-slider v6+, `'auto'` requires `slides: { perView: 'auto' }` and each slide needs explicit width CSS. The `.slide_auto_width` class in the catalog (lines 96–103 of `TrendingCarousel.jsx`) sets `maxWidth: '19.25rem'` — this hardcoded value would need to move somewhere in the v1 Carousel. The RFC does not address this at all.

**IntersectionObserver for `onView` — threshold and deduplication not specified.**
The catalog's view event fires at `threshold: 0.1`. The RFC's `onView` callback will presumably also use IntersectionObserver, but no threshold, rootMargin, or "fire once" behavior is specified. The catalog uses a `hasFiredViewEvent` ref. The RFC should specify this behavior to avoid each implementation making different choices.

---

## 4. Architecture

**Widgets living inside `packages/react/src/widgets/` creates an ownership ambiguity.**
The RFC puts the widget inside the Pigment package. But Section 3 ("Atoms Backfilling") says the Recommendations team owns and implements the `Atoms/` directory. Having an external team's code committed into Pigment's main package means Pigment's CI, release pipeline, and semver are now shared infrastructure for the Reco team's velocity. If the Reco team makes a breaking API change to their widget, it becomes a Pigment breaking change. The RFC does not address whether the widget is part of Pigment's versioned public API or an internal implementation detail. This is the most significant architectural risk and should have an explicit stance.

**The "fast-track" path has no exit criteria.**
Section 3 says the DS team "picks up the Jira tasks on their normal schedule." But there is no stated SLA, no condition under which a local `Atoms/` implementation gets promoted vs. diverges, and no mechanism to prevent the widget from being updated independently of the atoms once they're promoted. Concretely: if `RecommendationsProductCard` stays in `Atoms/` for 12 months while the DS team builds an official `ProductCard`, the widget and the atom will have different APIs. The RFC needs either a time-bound expectation or an ownership rule for resolving divergence.

**The `useTrendingProducts` hook being in Pigment is questionable.**
The hook's only logic is a thin `useEffect` fetch + cache wrapper. The catalog already uses React Query (`useTrendingProductsByCategory`), and the RFC itself shows the React Query alternative in the "Usage with React Query" example — which makes the hook completely optional. A hook that is acknowledged to be skippable by its primary consumer arguably does not belong in a shared component library. The RFC should either justify why it belongs in Pigment (generic enough that multiple apps benefit from the same impl) or recommend that catalog keeps its own hook. Publishing a hook that may be unused adds surface area without clear benefit.

**`recommendations-widgets/` directory name is kebab-case under `widgets/`.**
The repo convention (from `components.md`) is PascalCase for component folders. `recommendations-widgets` is kebab-case. All the atom folders under `Atoms/` are PascalCase. The intermediate namespace folder should follow a consistent convention. Either `RecommendationsWidgets/` or a flat `widgets/` structure without the intermediate folder.

---

## 5. Contradictions and Inconsistencies

**Section 2 (Widget decomposition) lists `FavoriteButton` as `[ ]` (unchecked) twice.**
Line: `- [ ] FavoriteButton - [ ]`
The trailing `[ ]` is a formatting artifact left over from editing. Minor but looks like incomplete work.

**Section 1 folder structure code fence has a mismatched Markdown fence.**
Lines 31–47: the opening ` ```plaintext ` is not closed before the `### 2. Widget decomposition` heading begins. Instead, the closing ` ``` ` appears *after* the `### 2.` heading and explanatory text. This renders the section header and decomposition text as part of the code block in Markdown. A reader on GitHub will see mangled output.

**Section "Assembled widget" usage example imports `FavoriteButton` from `@customink/pigment-react` (line 2 of the import block) but Section 4.4 places `FavoriteButton` in `Atoms/` — not the public package API.**
The RFC explicitly says atoms in `Atoms/` are "local, widget-scoped implementations — not part of the Pigment public API yet." But the catalog usage example imports it from the package root. These two statements contradict each other directly. If `FavoriteButton` is not yet public API, the catalog cannot import it that way.

**The `slidesPerView` table (Section "Assembled widget") says "Arrows shown only on desktop when `slidesPerView !== 'auto'`" but the catalog source code shows arrows are also suppressed on mobile regardless.**
`TrendingCarousel.jsx` line 230: `const showArrows = slidesPerView !== 'auto' && !isMobile;`
The RFC omits the `!isMobile` condition from its description. The described logic in the RFC is incomplete.

**The hook's `enabled` default is described as `@default true` in the JSDoc comment but the comment text reads "Skip fetching" which implies `@default false`.**
(Also noted in Technical Correctness — it is both a comment correctness issue and an internal contradiction.)

---

## 6. Naming

**`RecommendationsProductCard` is verbose and team-scoped.**
The component is spec'd as "a sub-variant of a future `ProductCard` atom." Naming it `RecommendationsProductCard` embeds team ownership into the component name. When the DS team promotes it to an official atom, a rename will be required. A name like `ProductCard` (with a variant or `compact` flag) or at minimum `ProductRecoCard` would make the eventual promotion less disruptive. The RFC itself says "Likely becomes a variant of a future `ProductCard` atom" — so it already anticipates a rename; it should minimize the distance from day-one.

**`recommendations-widgets/` as a directory name.**
As noted above, this is the only kebab-case folder in what is otherwise a PascalCase hierarchy. It should be `RecommendationsWidgets/` or simply removed in favor of putting `TrendingProductsWidget/` directly under `widgets/`.

**`WidgetViewedGA4Event` and `CardClickedGA4Event` type names.**
The "Viewed" vs "Clicked" asymmetry is slightly odd. More importantly these type names contain "GA4" which is an analytics vendor detail. If analytics ever migrate to a different platform, the type name becomes misleading. `WidgetViewEvent` and `CardClickEvent` or — more directly following GA4 naming conventions — `ViewItemListEvent` and `SelectItemEvent` would be cleaner.

**`favoriteSlot` prop on `RecommendationsProductCard`.**
The existing Pigment pattern (from `Button.tsx`) uses `startIcon`, `endIcon` — descriptive of position or role. The name `favoriteSlot` is functional/semantic. Consider `imageOverlay` or `cardOverlay` which describes the position and is not tied to the specific use case of favorites. This makes the component more reusable if a different overlay (e.g. a badge or a promo tag) is needed later.
